value.body:
## Problem

The initial ticket implementation turn is finalized by Cycle, but later human-guided turns can leave valid UI changes only in the managed worktree. The agent may then try to reason about direct Git operations even though the assignment prompt says Cycle owns commit and push.

The result is contradictory user feedback: the assistant says no commit/push occurred, while a post-response workflow may publish a commit; or the assistant reports successful code changes before the workflow later fails.

## Desired behavior

Every successful turn in a `ticket-implementation` thread—initial work and review feedback alike—must run the same authoritative managed handover. The user should see a completed response only after commit and required push outcomes are known.

## Implementation detail

- Invoke the `ticket-implementation` completion workflow after every completed task belonging to an implementation thread.
- Use `WorktreeFinalizer`, `WorktreeBranchPublisher`, and `WorktreeRemotePublisher`; do not require the provider to run `git add`, `git commit`, `git switch`, or `git push`.
- Keep managed worktrees detached if that is the lifecycle invariant; publish/update the stable ticket branch through the branch publisher.
- For a follow-up turn:
  1. Inspect the worktree and calculate the delta since the last handed-over SHA.
  2. Create a commit only for the completed turn's intended changes.
  3. Update the existing ticket branch association without creating a competing branch.
  4. Push with the configured required policy.
  5. Persist commit SHA, remote name/ref, push timestamp, and handed-over SHA.
  6. Add/update the ticket handoff and transition back to review when appropriate.
- Separate provider output from task terminal state. Introduce a `finalizing`/handover phase so the task is not shown as completed while publishing is still running.
- Return a structured completion resource to the frontend containing implementation summary, validation, commit(s), branch, remote ref/link, push outcome, and any typed failure.
- If no source delta exists, record an explicit no-op handover rather than creating an empty commit.
- If push fails, retain the worktree and local branch, mark the task as publish-blocked/retryable, and expose a retry-publish action.
- Ensure branch-association `pushedAt` and remote fields are updated atomically with the successful push receipt.
- Make direct Git write permission irrelevant to normal agent operation; the trusted Cycle lifecycle service owns publication.

## Acceptance criteria

- Every successful implementation follow-up produces either a published commit or an explicit no-op handover.
- The same stable ticket branch is updated across multiple feedback rounds.
- The frontend never displays a terminal success before handover completion.
- User-visible completion includes the exact commit and remote branch/push status.
- A push failure does not discard changes or falsely report success.
- Retry publication is idempotent and cannot duplicate commits or ticket comments.
- Direct provider Git writes are not needed for the standard workflow.
- Branch association and handover records agree on head SHA, remote ref, and pushed timestamp.

## Tests

- Integration test: initial implementation plus two feedback turns creates three ordered commits on one branch and pushes each.
- Failure injection for commit, branch update, authentication, DNS/timeout, and non-fast-forward push.
- Process-restart test proving an in-progress handover resumes idempotently.
- Contract/UI test for finalizing, published, publish-blocked, and no-op states.
- Regression test matching the historical `UKN-WB555` follow-up sequence.

## Non-goals

- Creating pull requests.
- Letting the provider bypass Cycle's branch and handover ownership.

frontmatter.title: Run managed commit and push handover after every implementation follow-up

value.title: Run managed commit and push handover after every implementation follow-up
